title: XLNet: Generalized Autoregressive Pretraining for Language Understanding
authors: Zhilin Yang, Zihang Dai, Yiming Yang, Jaime Carbonell et al.
hook: XLNet reads language both ways, learning from all possible word orders at once.
promise: You will understand how XLNet unifies bidirectional context with autoregressive training to outperform BERT.
prerequisite_note: You only need a sense of how language models guess missing words from surrounding context.

glossary.plain: A way of predicting the next word in a sequence, using only previous words.
Using both left and right context around a word to understand its meaning.
Training by randomly changing the order in which words are predicted, so the model learns from all positions.
The problem where a model is trained with artificial masks but tested without them, causing a gap between training and real use.
A neural network that can remember longer sequences in language tasks, used as a base for XLNet.

cards.title: Learn from all sides
body: Discover how XLNet combines the strengths of BERT and autoregressive models for deeper language understanding.

cards.title: Why just one direction?
body: Imagine trying to guess a missing word in a sentence. You would want to look at both the words before and after it.
Early language models only looked forward or backward, missing out on richer context. XLNet set out to break this limit.

cards.title: How XLNet learns from every angle
body: First, XLNet takes a sentence and considers every possible order in which the words could be predicted.
Then, it learns to predict each word, always using all available context, sometimes from the left, sometimes from the right.
In the end, XLNet becomes skilled at capturing dependencies from any direction, not just one.
cards.caption: XLNet maximizes learning by treating every prediction order as a training opportunity.

cards.title: Masking versus permutation
body: See how predicting words differs when you mask them out or change prediction order.
cards.offNote: Masks break the flow, so dependencies between masked words are lost.
cards.onNote: Permutations let the model learn all dependencies, no matter the word position.

cards.title: The permutation trick
body: XLNet avoids masking words. Instead, it shuffles the order in which it predicts them, letting the model see every possible context.
This generalizes the autoregressive idea, training on all possible word orders, so the model never misses a connection between words.

cards.title: How much context does the model use?
body: Slide to see how the amount of context available changes model power.
cards.low: Model sees only previous words, missing future context.
cards.mid: Model sees some left and right context, but not all dependencies.
cards.high: Model uses all possible context, capturing every dependency.

cards.title: Pick XLNet's key ingredients
body: Choose which elements make XLNet unique compared to older models.

choices.label: Permutation language modeling
choices.note: Lets the model learn from every word order.

choices.label: No input masking
choices.note: Removes the train-test mismatch found in BERT.

choices.label: Autoregressive prediction
choices.note: Predicts one word at a time, using context.

choices.label: Bidirectional context
choices.note: Uses both left and right context for each prediction.

choices.label: Transformer-XL memory
choices.note: Remembers longer sequences for better predictions.

choices.label: Expected likelihood objective
choices.note: Maximizes the chance of predicting all possible permutations.

cards.title: XLNet's impact on benchmarks
body: When tested on 20 language tasks, XLNet outperformed BERT in nearly every case, often by a large margin.
Tasks like question answering, sentiment analysis, and document ranking all saw clear gains with XLNet.
cards.caption: XLNet set new records on many standard language benchmarks.

cards.title: A new standard for context
body: XLNet proved that you do not need to mask words to learn from all directions, breaking a core limitation of BERT.
Its permutation-based approach inspired later models and set a new bar for what language understanding models could achieve.

recap.label: Shuffle
recap.note: Permute the order of word predictions.

recap.label: Predict
recap.note: Guess each word using all context.

recap.label: Remember
recap.note: Use Transformer-XL memory for longer sequences.

recap.label: Outperform
recap.note: Beat previous models on language benchmarks.

cards.title: XLNet: Generalized Autoregressive Pretraining for Language Understanding
body: Explore the original paper for technical details and experimental results.
